provisions.program_name: Medicare Part D cost-sharing reductions for low-income individuals
provisions.statutory_reference: Section 1860D–14(a) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–114(a))
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text: Section 1860D–14(a) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–114(a)) is amended— (1) paragraph (1)(D)(ii), by striking ''that does not exceed $1 for'' and all that fol
notes: Restructures cost-sharing for low-income Part D beneficiaries in two phases
Before 2028: maintains $1 copay for generics/preferred multiple source drugs, $3 for other drugs (or lesser copayment amount)
2028 and after: sets generic copay to $0; preferred multiple source drug copay and other drug copays indexed to CPI (September of prior year)
Establishes rounding rules: amounts based on $1 increase rounded to nearest 5 cents; amounts based on $3 increase rounded to nearest 10 cents
Modifies paragraph (4)(A)(ii) to limit 'subsequent year' increases to years before 2028

provisions.description: Establishes comprehensive Medicare Advantage provider directory accuracy requirements effective plan year 2028, including: (1) requiring specified MA plans to maintain accurate publicly available provider directories and verify information at least every 90 days; (2) requiring in-network cost-sharing protections for enrollees who rely on inaccurate provider directory listings; (3) mandating annual provider directory accuracy analysis with random sampling and reporting to the Secretary; (4) requiring public posting of accuracy scores beginning plan year 2029; and (5) requiring enrollee notification of cost-sharing protections.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text: Section 1852(c) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–22(c)) is amended— (1) paragraph (1)(C)— (A) by striking ''plan, and any'' and inserting ''plan, any''; and
notes: Applies to 'specified MA plans': network-based plans and PFFS plans meeting access standards through contracts
Directory must include: name, specialty, contact info, addresses, new patient acceptance, disability accommodations, cultural/linguistic capabilities, telehealth capabilities
Hospitals and other facilities may have verification frequency less than 90 days but not less than every 12 months
Providers must be removed within 5 business days if no longer participating
Cost-sharing protection: enrollee pays lesser of in-network or out-of-network cost-sharing when relying on inaccurate directory
Accuracy analysis must include random sample with emphasis on specialties with high inaccuracy rates, which may include mental health/SUD providers
Secretary may waive requirements for low-enrollment specified MA plans
Accuracy scores posted in machine-readable format beginning plan year 2029

Criterion 1: FDA-approved prescribing information instructs administration by or under supervision of a health care professional

provisions.description: Amends Section 1860D-12 of the Social Security Act to add new subsection (h) establishing comprehensive requirements for pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) acting on behalf of PDP sponsors under Medicare Part D, effective for plan years beginning on or after January 1, 2028. Requirements include: (1) PBMs may not derive remuneration other than bona fide service fees; (2) transparency requirements regarding pricing guarantees and cost performance evaluations; (3) detailed annual reporting to PDP sponsors and the Secretary on drug costs, rebates, reimbursement, affiliates, and related information; (4) audit rights for PDP sponsors; (5) enforcement including disgorgement, civil money penalties, and anti-retaliation protections; (6) certification of compliance; and (7) confidentiality protections for disclosed information.

provisions.description: Requires the Comptroller General (GAO) to conduct a study describing the use of compensation and payment structures related to a prescription drug's price within the retail prescription drug supply chain in Medicare Part D. The study must summarize information from Federal agencies and industry experts regarding price-related compensation structures among PBMs, PDP sponsors, drug wholesalers, pharmacies, manufacturers, pharmacy services administrative organizations, brokers, auditors, consultants, and other entities. The study must also address business models, variation between affiliated and unaffiliated entities, potential conflicts of interest, differences across market segments and over time, and effects on Federal health care programs and beneficiaries.
deadlines: 2 years after the date of enactment

notes: Subsection (a). Inserts new clause (xi) establishing a funding formula for FY2026-2040.
Formula: previous fiscal year amount × 1.07 × (enrollment on July 1 of previous FY / enrollment on July 1 of year before that).
No fixed dollar amount — funding level is determined by the formula each year based on 7% growth rate adjusted for enrollment changes.
Redesignates existing clause (xi) as (xii).
Subtitle B heading: 'World Trade Center Health Program'.

provisions.description: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to conduct an assessment of anticipated budget authority and outlays of the World Trade Center Health Program through its duration and submit a report to congressional leaders and committees within 3 years of enactment.
deadlines: 3 years after the date of enactment
provisions.title: IV
provisions.raw_text: Not later than 3 years after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary of Health and Human Services (referred to in this subsection as the ''Secretary'') shall conduct an assess
notes: Subsection (b). Report must include: (A) projection of budgetary needs per fiscal year through FY2090; (B) review of modeling for FY2017 through prior year; (C) comparison of projected authority/expenditures under the formula vs. hypothetical extension through FY2090; (D) any recommendations to change the formula to offset anticipated expenditures through FY2090.
Report recipients: Speaker and minority leader of the House; majority and minority leaders of the Senate; Senate HELP and Budget Committees; House Energy and Commerce and Budget Committees.

provisions.description: Amends Section 372 of the Public Health Service Act regarding the Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network. Adds requirements for electronic health records integration among hospitals, OPOs, and transplant centers; a public dashboard of transplant statistics; and new registration fee authority for OPTN members. Registration fees remain available until expended, are credited as discretionary offsetting collections, and include transparency and GAO review requirements. Fee authority sunsets 3 years after enactment.

provisions.description: Directs the Secretary of HHS to arrange with the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to convene an expert committee within 30 days to study premature births, including financial costs, contributing factors, detection of risk factors, and targeted research strategies. Report due within 24 months of enactment to Congress.
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: The Secretary of Health and Human Services shall enter into appropriate arrangements with the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine under which the National Academies shall—
notes: Subsection (c) of SEC. 6507
Study must assess: financial costs of premature birth, factors impacting preterm birth rates, opportunities for earlier detection of risk factors
Analysis must cover targeted research strategies, state best practices, and precision medicine approaches
Report must include raw data and be approved by consensus
Report to be transmitted to Secretary of HHS, House Energy and Commerce Committee, and Senate Finance and HELP Committees

provisions.description: Amends Section 505B(a)(3) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to expand molecularly targeted pediatric cancer investigation requirements to include combination therapies with additional active ingredients, and clarifies applicability of pediatric study requirements under subparagraphs (A) and (B) of section 505B(a)(1). Amendments apply to applications submitted 3 years or more after enactment.

provisions.description: Amends Section 527 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to narrow orphan drug exclusivity from the 'same disease or condition' to the 'same approved use or indication within such rare disease or condition,' and adds a definition of 'approved use or indication.' Amendments apply retroactively to any drug designated under section 526 regardless of designation or approval date.
provisions.title: VI
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 6605. 
Limitations on exclusive approval or licensure of orphan drugs

(a) 
In general
Section 527 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 360cc) is amended
notes: Subtitle A (inferred from section numbering)
Narrows orphan drug exclusivity scope: changes 'same disease or condition' to 'same approved use or indication within such rare disease or condition'
Retroactive application regardless of date of designation or approval/licensure
Adds new subsection (f) defining 'approved use or indication'

cross_references.target: Section 527 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 360cc)

provisions.description: Establishes the Abraham Accords Office within the Food and Drug Administration, headed by a director, to be located in an Abraham Accords country within 2 years. The Office shall provide technical assistance to regulatory partners in Abraham Accords countries on regulatory oversight and manufacturing of FDA-regulated products, and facilitate interactions between FDA and interested parties in those countries.
provisions.title: VI
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 6611. 
Establishment of Abraham Accords Office within Food and Drug Administration
(a) 
In general
Chapter X of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 391 et seq.) is amended
notes: Subtitle B — United States-Abraham Accords Cooperation and Security
Adds new SEC. 1015 to Chapter X of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act
Office must be established within 2 years of enactment in a location selected in consultation with Abraham Accords country governments and U.S. diplomatic/security personnel
'Abraham Accords country' defined as a country identified by the Department of State as having signed the Abraham Accords Declaration
Includes national security savings clause: nothing requires action inconsistent with national security recommendations

cross_references.target: Chapter X of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 391 et seq.)
cross_references.description: Adds new Section 1015 establishing the Abraham Accords Office

provisions.description: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to submit a report to Congress within 3 years on the Abraham Accords Office, including evaluation of progress toward FDA regulatory conformance by manufacturers in Abraham Accords countries, count of facilitated interactions, summary of technical assistance, and recommendations for improving coordination.

provisions.raw_text:
(b) 
Report to Congress
(1) 
In general

notes: Subsection (b) of SEC. 6611

provisions.description: Amends Title XXVII of the Public Health Service Act by adding new section 2799A-11 to establish oversight of entities providing pharmacy benefit management (PBM) services. Prohibits group health plans, health insurance issuers, and PBM entities from contracting with applicable entities unless those entities agree not to limit disclosure of information and to provide relevant information for required reports. Requires PBM entities to submit semiannual (or quarterly at plan request) reports to group health plans containing detailed drug-level and therapeutic-class-level cost, spending, rebate, and utilization data. Establishes separate reporting tiers for specified large employers/plans (detailed claims-level data) and all group health plans (summary documents). Requires plans to provide information to participants and beneficiaries upon request. Sets civil monetary penalties of $10,000 per day for failure to disclose/report and up to $100,000 per item of false information. Requires the Secretary to promulgate rulemaking on standard reporting format and other implementing regulations within 18 months. Includes privacy requirements consistent with HIPAA and HITECH. Also amends section 2723 of the PHSA to exclude new section 2799A-11 from certain state enforcement provisions.

provisions.description: Establishes comprehensive oversight and transparency requirements for entities providing pharmacy benefit management (PBM) services on behalf of group health plans and health insurance issuers. Requires PBM entities to report detailed drug cost, spending, rebate, and compensation information to group health plans at least semiannually. Requires plans to provide summary information to participants and beneficiaries upon request. Amends the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 by adding new section 726, amends ERISA section 502 to add enforcement authority, and amends ERISA section 732. Also amends Chapter 100 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986.
provisions.policy_area: Health care / pharmacy benefit management transparency
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 726. 
Oversight of entities that provide pharmacy benefit management services
(a) 
In general
For plan years beginning on or after the date that is 30 months after the dat
notes: Effective for plan years beginning 30 months after enactment
Applies to group health plans and health insurance issuers offering group health insurance coverage
Subsection (a) prohibits contracts with applicable entities unless they agree to disclosure and information-sharing requirements
Subsection (b) requires semiannual (or quarterly upon request) reports from PBM entities to group health plans with detailed drug-level and therapeutic-class-level cost/spending/rebate information
Subsection (b)(2)(A) applies to specified large employers (100+ employees) or specified large plans (100+ participants) — includes extensive per-drug and per-therapeutic-class data
Subsection (b)(2)(B) applies to all group health plans — includes summary documents and aggregate information
Subsection (b)(4) imposes privacy requirements consistent with HIPAA and HITECH Act regulations
Subsection (b)(5) requires Secretary to promulgate rulemaking within 18 months of enactment for standard reporting format and implementation regulations
Subsection (c) requires plans to provide information to participants/beneficiaries upon request
Subsection (d) rule of construction preserving Secretary's access to reports
Subsection (e) defines key terms including applicable entity, contracted compensation, gross spending, net spending, specified large employer, specified large plan, and wholesale acquisition cost
Enforcement penalties: $10,000/day for failure to provide required information; up to $100,000 per item of false information (added to ERISA section 502(c)(13))
Secretary may waive penalties for good-faith compliance efforts
Also amends ERISA section 732(a) to include section 726 in preemption provisions
Also amends Internal Revenue Code Chapter 100 subchapter B (text appears truncated)
This is a policy rider — no appropriation of funds

provisions.description: Adds new IRC Section 9826 establishing comprehensive oversight requirements for entities providing pharmacy benefit management services on behalf of group health plans. Prohibits group health plans from contracting with applicable entities unless they agree not to limit or delay disclosure of information and to provide relevant information for reports. Requires PBM entities to submit semiannual reports (or quarterly upon plan request) to group health plans containing detailed drug-level and therapeutic-class-level spending, rebate, compensation, formulary, and affiliated-pharmacy information. Includes privacy requirements consistent with HIPAA regulations, written notice obligations, restrictions on public disclosure, and requirements for the Secretary to issue standard format and implementing regulations within 18 months of enactment. Effective for plan years beginning 30 months after enactment.

provisions.description: Applies a temporary exemption from FLSA overtime requirements (section 7) for employees engaged in adjusting or evaluating claims resulting from a major disaster for a period of 2 years after the disaster, subject to conditions including minimum weekly compensation of $591.00, licensing requirements, and employer maintaining workers' compensation and tax withholding.
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 108. (a) Section 7 of the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 (29 U.S.C. 207) shall be applied as if the following text is part of such section:
notes: Applies FLSA section 7 as if new subsection (s) were part of the statute — a temporary legislative overlay, not a permanent amendment
Exemption applies for 2 years after occurrence of a major disaster
Minimum weekly compensation threshold: $591.00 or any higher amount established by the Secretary
Employee must be licensed, employer must maintain workers' comp and withhold taxes
Employer must not be engaged in underwriting, selling, or marketing insurance
Does not affect exemption under section 13(a)(1) of FLSA
Major disaster defined as any disaster declared or designated by any State or Federal agency
Effective on date of enactment

cross_references.target: Section 7 of the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 (29 U.S.C. 207)
cross_references.description: Applied as if new subsection (s) is part of the section — temporary overlay

provisions.description: Provides flexibility for employers in the seafood industry to bring H-2B nonimmigrants into the United States at any time during a 120-day period beginning on the petition start date without filing another petition. After 90 days, employer must conduct a new local labor market assessment and offer the job to qualified U.S. workers. Exempts such staggered crossings from DOL staggering regulations.
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 109. (a) Flexibility with respect to the crossing of H–2B nonimmigrants working in the seafood industry (1) In general Subject to paragraph (2),
notes: 120-day window from petition start date for crossings
After 90 days: new local labor market assessment required (newspaper listings on 2 Sundays, job posting on DOL registry and at employer)
Must offer job to equally or better qualified U.S. workers who apply and are available
Exempts seafood employers from staggering violation under 20 CFR 655.20(d)
H-2B nonimmigrants defined by reference to INA section 101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B)

cross_references.target: Section 101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B) of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B))

cross_references.description: Staggering regulation from which seafood employers are exempted

provisions.description: Sets the methodology for determining prevailing wage for H-2B program purposes as the greater of the actual employer wage for similar employees or the prevailing wage for the occupation in the geographic area. Requires the Secretary to accept private wage surveys even when OES data are available unless the Secretary determines the survey methodology and data are not statistically supported.
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 110. The determination of prevailing wage for the purposes of the H–2B program shall be the greater of—(1) the actual wage level paid by the empl
notes: Two-pronged test: actual employer wage vs. prevailing occupational wage in geographic area
Private wage surveys must be accepted unless methodology/data are not statistically supported
This effectively limits DOL's discretion to reject private wage survey data

provisions.description: Amends the Act creating the Department of Labor (37 Stat. 736) to authorize the Secretary of Labor to employ law enforcement officers or special agents to provide protection for the Secretary, immediate family members under threat, and senior officials at public events. provisions.description: Prohibits the use of any funds appropriated in this Act, or in any trust fund to which funds are appropriated in this Act, for any abortion or for health benefits coverage that includes coverage of abortion (Hyde Amendment).
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (a) None of the funds appropriated in this Act, and none of the funds in any trust fund to which funds are appropriated in this Act, shall be expended
notes: Standard Hyde Amendment language
Subsection (a) prohibits funding for abortions
Subsection (b) prohibits funding for health benefits coverage that includes abortion coverage
Subsection (c) defines 'health benefits coverage' as the package of services covered by a managed care provider/organization

provisions.description: Provides exceptions to the Hyde Amendment for rape, incest, and life-endangering physical conditions; clarifies state/local funds may still be used; and prohibits discrimination against health care entities that do not provide, pay for, or refer for abortions (conscience protection).
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (a) The limitations established in the preceding section shall not apply to an abortion— (1) if the pregnancy is the result of an act of rape or ince
notes: Subsection (a) provides Hyde exceptions for rape/incest and life-endangering physical conditions certified by a physician
Subsection (b) clarifies that state/local/private funds (excluding Medicaid matching) may still be used
Subsection (c) clarifies managed care providers may offer abortion coverage with state funds (excluding Medicaid matching)

Subsection (d) is a conscience/non-discrimination provision—prohibits funds to any agency, program, or government that discriminates against health care entities for not providing/paying for/referring for abortions
Subsection (d)(2) broadly defines 'health care entity' to include individual physicians, hospitals, HMOs, insurance plans, etc.

cross_references.target: SEC. 506
cross_references.description: The preceding section establishing Hyde Amendment limitations

notes: Standard Dickey-Wicker Amendment language

provisions.description: Restricts reprogramming of funds provided under this Act, prior appropriations, or fee-derived accounts without advance consultation with and written notification to the Committees on Appropriations. Subsection (a) covers structural reprogramming (new programs, eliminations, denied items, relocations, reorganizations, privatization) with 15-day consultation and 10-day written notice. Subsection (b) covers reprogramming in excess of $500,000 or 10 percent (whichever is less) for augmentations, 10% reductions, or personnel-related savings with the same notification requirements.
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (a) None of the funds provided under this Act, or provided under previous appropriations Acts to the agencies funded by this Act that remain available
notes: Subsection (a) requires consultation 15 days and written notice 10 days in advance for reprogramming that creates/eliminates programs, increases denied items, relocates offices/employees, reorganizes, or privatizes
Subsection (b) sets $500,000 or 10% threshold (whichever is less) for reprogramming that augments programs, reduces funding/personnel by 10%, or results from general personnel savings
Applies to current-year funds, prior-year carryover, and fee-derived accounts

provisions.description: Establishes the methodology for distributing the obligation limitation for Federal-aid highways for FY2026, including formulas for distribution to States, exceptions from the limitation, redistribution of unused authority after August 1, applicability to transportation research programs, and redistribution of funds not allocated due to the limitation.
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: (a) For fiscal year 2026, the Secretary of Transportation shall— (1) not distribute from the obligation limitation for Federal-aid highways—
notes: Standard annual provision governing distribution of Federal-aid highway obligation limitation
Subsection (a) sets distribution methodology: excludes admin expenses and BTS, accounts for unobligated balances, and distributes proportionally
Subsection (b) lists 12 categories exempt from the obligation limitation, including emergency relief (sec. 125 of title 23) and $639,000,000/year for NHPP under section 119
Subsection (b)(8), (10), and (12) each reference $639,000,000 as amounts exempt from obligation limitation
Subsection (c) requires redistribution of unused obligation authority after August 1
Subsection (d) applies obligation limitation to research programs under chapter 5 of title 23, FAST Act Title VI, and IIJA Title III of Division A
Subsection (e) requires distribution of unallocated funds within 30 days, available for section 133(b) purposes

Extends non-competitive renewals to Q2 if fiscal year 2025 NOFO awards not made by April 1, 2026, and to Q3/Q4 if not made by July 1, 2026. Renewals shall be at prior award levels with upward adjustments for cost-of-living and fair market rent changes. Remaining amounts shall be competitively awarded. Renewals do not render recipients ineligible for FY2025/FY2026 NOFOs.

provisions.description: Establishes reprogramming restrictions for all agencies and entities funded in this Act. Prohibits reprogramming that: creates a new program; eliminates a program/project/activity; increases funds denied by Congress; redirects specifically directed funds; augments or reduces programs by more than $5,000,000 or 10 percent (whichever is less); or creates/reorganizes offices. Requires each agency to submit an operating plan within 60 days of enactment. Agencies may reprogram beyond thresholds only after 30-day advance written notice and prior written approval from Appropriations Committees.

provisions.description: Prohibits any department, agency, or instrumentality receiving FY2026 appropriated funds from obligating or expending funds unless it has and administers a written drug-free workplace policy to ensure all workplaces are free from illegal use, possession, or distribution of controlled substances.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 701. No department, agency, or instrumentality of the United States receiving appropriated funds under this or any other Act for fiscal year 2026 shall obligate or expend any such funds, unless such department, agency, or instrumentality has in place, and will continue to administer in good faith, a written policy designed to ensure that all of its workplaces are free from the illegal use, possession, or distribution of controlled substances
notes: Government-wide drug-free workplace requirement; applies to all FY2026 appropriated funds under this or any other Act

cross_references.target: Controlled Substances Act (21 U.S.C. 802)

provisions.description: Fixes the maximum allowable purchase price for passenger motor vehicles at $40,000 (station wagons at $41,140) for FY2026, with exceptions for police-type vehicles (up to $7,775 over limit), electric/hybrid demonstration vehicles (up to 5% over limit), clean alternative fuel vehicles (incremental cost over conventional), and commercial alternative fuel vehicles (no limit).
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 702. Unless otherwise specifically provided, the maximum amount allowable during the current fiscal year in accordance with section 1343(c) of title 31, United States Code, for the purchase of any passenger motor vehicle (exclusive of buses, ambulances, vans, law enforcement vehicles, protective vehicles, undercover surveillance vehicles, and police-type vehicles), is hereby fixed at $40,000
notes: Base limit: $40,000 for passenger vehicles, $41,140 for station wagons
Police-type vehicles may exceed by up to $7,775
Electric/hybrid demo vehicles may exceed by up to 5%
Clean alternative fuel vehicles may exceed by incremental cost over conventional
Commercial alternative fuel vehicles are fully exempt from limits
Excludes buses, ambulances, vans, law enforcement vehicles, protective vehicles, undercover surveillance vehicles, and police-type vehicles from the base limit

cross_references.description: Statutory basis for vehicle purchase limits

provisions.description: Makes executive department and independent establishment appropriations available for current fiscal year travel expenses also available for quarters allowances and cost-of-living allowances in accordance with 5 U.S.C. 5922-5924.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 703. Appropriations of the executive departments and independent establishments for the current fiscal year available for expenses of travel, or for the expenses of the activity concerned, are hereby made available for quarters allowances and cost-of-living allowances, in accordance with 5 U.S.C. 5922–5924.
notes: Standard government-wide provision extending travel appropriations to cover quarters and cost-of-living allowances

cross_references.description: Statutory authority for quarters and cost-of-living allowances

provisions.description: Prohibits use of appropriated funds to pay compensation of any federal officer or employee whose post of duty is in the continental United States unless the person is a U.S. citizen, lawful permanent resident seeking citizenship, refugee/asylee who has filed for permanent residency, or a person who owes allegiance to the United States. Includes exceptions for current employees, international broadcasters, translators, temporary field service emergency employees (up to 60 days), and wildland firefighters employed by DOI or USDA Forest Service (up to 120 days) pursuant to international agreements.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 704. Unless otherwise specified in law during the current fiscal year, no part of any appropriation contained in this or any other Act shall be used to pay the compensation of any officer or employee of the Government of the United States (including any agency the majority of the stock of which is owned by the Government of the United States) whose post of duty is in the continental United States unless such person
notes: False affidavit is a felony punishable by up to $4,000 fine and/or 1 year imprisonment
Exceptions: current employees as of enactment, BBG international broadcasters, translators, temporary field service (60 days), wildland firefighters (120 days via international agreement)
Standard government-wide citizenship/immigration status requirement

provisions.description: Makes department and agency appropriations for necessary expenses (including maintenance and operating expenses) available for payment to GSA for space, services, and renovation/alteration of buildings and facilities constituting public improvements under the Public Buildings Act of 1959, Public Buildings Amendments of 1972, or other applicable law.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 705. Appropriations available to any department or agency during the current fiscal year for necessary expenses, including maintenance or operating expenses, shall also be available for payment to the General Services Administration for charges for space and services and those expenses of renovation and alteration of buildings and facilities
notes: Standard government-wide provision authorizing GSA payments from existing appropriations

cross_references.target: Public Buildings Act of 1959 (73 Stat. 479)
Public Buildings Amendments of 1972 (86 Stat. 216)

provisions.description: Authorizes all federal agencies to receive and use funds from the sale of materials (including disposed federal records) recovered through recycling or waste prevention programs. Funds are available until expended for: (1) acquisition, waste reduction/prevention, and recycling programs; (2) environmental management programs including hazardous waste and pollution prevention; (3) other employee programs as authorized by law or deemed appropriate by the agency head.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 706. In addition to funds provided in this or any other Act, all Federal agencies are authorized to receive and use funds resulting from the sale of materials, including Federal records disposed of pursuant to a records schedule recovered through recycling or waste prevention programs. Such funds shall be available until expended
notes: Not a traditional appropriation—authorizes retention and use of recycling/waste prevention proceeds
Available until expended (no-year availability)
References Executive Order No. 14057 (December 8, 2021) for recycling programs

cross_references.target: Executive Order No. 14057 (December 8, 2021)

provisions.description: Makes funds available for administrative expenses of corporations and agencies subject to chapter 91 of title 31 also available for rent in DC, personal services contracts under 5 U.S.C. 3109, and other specified objects. Includes a proviso that administrative expense limitations shall be correspondingly reduced if functions are transferred to or paid from other funds.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 707. Funds made available by this or any other Act for administrative expenses in the current fiscal year of the corporations and agencies subject to chapter 91 of title 31, United States Code, shall be available, in addition to objects for which such funds are otherwise available, for rent in the District of Columbia; services in accordance with 5 U.S.C. 3109
notes: Applies to government corporations subject to chapter 91 of title 31 (Government Corporation Control Act)

cross_references.target: chapter 91 of title 31, United States Code

provisions.description: Prohibits use of any appropriated funds for interagency financing of boards, commissions, councils, committees, or similar groups that do not have prior and specific statutory approval to receive financial support from more than one agency. Excepts Federal Executive Boards.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 708. No part of any appropriation contained in this or any other Act shall be available for interagency financing of boards (except Federal Executive Boards), commissions, councils, committees, or similar groups (whether or not they are interagency entities) which do not have a prior and specific statutory approval to receive financial support from more than one agency or instrumentality.
notes: Federal Executive Boards are excepted from this prohibition

provisions.description: Prohibits use of any appropriated funds to implement, administer, or enforce any regulation that has been disapproved pursuant to a joint resolution under applicable law (i.e., the Congressional Review Act).
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 709. None of the funds made available pursuant to the provisions of this or any other Act shall be used to implement, administer, or enforce any regulation which has been disapproved pursuant to a joint resolution duly adopted in accordance with the applicable law of the United States.
notes: Effectively codifies the spending prohibition associated with Congressional Review Act disapprovals

provisions.description: Prohibits obligation or expenditure of more than $5,000 to furnish, redecorate, or purchase furniture/improvements for the office of any presidential appointee (department/agency heads, officers, or civilian employees) unless advance notice is transmitted to the House and Senate Appropriations Committees. 'Office' includes entire suite and any space primarily used by or controlled by the individual.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 710. During the period in which the head of any department or agency, or any other officer or civilian employee of the Federal Government appointed by the President of the United States, holds office, no funds may be obligated or expended in excess of $5,000 to furnish or redecorate the office of such department head, agency head, officer, or employee
notes: $5,000 threshold for congressional notification, not a hard cap—advance notice to Appropriations Committees required above that amount

provisions.description: Notwithstanding 31 U.S.C. 1346 and section 708 of this Act, allows funds made available by this or any other Act for the current fiscal year to be used for interagency funding of national security and emergency preparedness telecommunications initiatives that benefit multiple federal departments, agencies, or entities, as provided by Executive Order No. 13618.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 711. Notwithstanding 31 U.S.C. 1346, or section 708 of this Act, funds made available for the current fiscal year by this or any other Act shall be available for the interagency funding of national security and emergency preparedness telecommunications initiatives which benefit multiple Federal departments, agencies, or entities, as provided by Executive Order No. 13618 (July 6, 2012).
notes: Overrides the interagency financing prohibition in SEC. 708 and 31 U.S.C. 1346 for national security telecom

cross_references.target: SEC. 708 of this Act
cross_references.description: Appropriations Act prohibition on interagency financing

cross_references.target: Executive Order No. 13618 (July 6, 2012)
cross_references.description: Assignment of National Security and Emergency Preparedness Communications Functions

provisions.description: Prohibits any department, agency, or instrumentality from obligating or expending funds to pay salaries or expenses of any Schedule C appointee unless the employing agency head certifies to the OPM Director that the Schedule C position was not created solely or primarily to detail the individual to the White House. Exception for intelligence community personnel.
provisions.title: VII
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 712. 
(a) None of the funds made available by this or any other Act may be obligated or expended by any department, agency, or other instrumentality of the Federal Government to pay the salaries or expenses of any individual appointed to a position of a confidential or policy-determining character that is excepted from the competitive service under section 3302 of title 5
notes: Targets Schedule C positions (5 CFR part 213, subpart C)

provisions.description: Restricts reprogramming of Federal funds and DC government agency funds for FY2026 without prior approval from the Committees on Appropriations. Prohibits reprogramming that creates new programs, eliminates programs, changes specifically denied allocations, increases denied/restricted funds, re-establishes deferred programs, augments programs by more than $3,000,000 or 10 percent (whichever is less), or increases personnel by 20 percent or more. Authorizes DC government to approve and execute reprogramming/transfer requests of local funds through November 7, 2026.

provisions.description: Amends the District of Columbia College Access Act of 1999 to increase maximum annual tuition payments from $10,000 to $15,000 and aggregate lifetime caps from $50,000 to $75,000 for public institutions (section 3), and from $2,500 to $3,750 annually and $12,500 to $18,750 in aggregate for private institutions (section 5). Also adds a ratable reduction mechanism for students receiving above certain thresholds.
provisions.policy_area: District of Columbia higher education tuition assistance
provisions.title: VIII
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 820. The District of Columbia College Access Act of 1999 (sec. 38–2701 et seq., D.C. Official Code), is amended— 
(1) in specti
notes: Section 3 amendments (public institutions): annual cap raised from $10,000 to $15,000; aggregate cap raised from $50,000 to $75,000; adds ratable reduction for students receiving more than $10,000 per award year

Section 5 amendments (private institutions): annual cap raised from $2,500 to $3,750; aggregate cap raised from $12,500 to $18,750; adds ratable reduction for students receiving more than $2,500 per award year
New subparagraph (B) in each section establishes a tiered reduction mechanism

provisions.description: Funds shall be available only to voluntary family planning projects which offer a broad range of family planning methods/services and meet 5 requirements: (1) no quotas or numerical targets; (2) no incentives, bribes, gratuities, or financial rewards for acceptors or personnel; (3) no denial of rights/benefits for non-acceptance; (4) comprehensible information on health benefits and risks of chosen method; (5) experimental drugs/devices only in scientific studies with informed consent. Secretary of State must report violations to Appropriations Committees within 60 days.
